summary: Joe Reis argues that AI initiatives have historically failed because organizations skipped foundational work—quality data, proper engineering, and clear problem framing—in favor of hype-driven investments. Drawing on his experience in ML startups during the 2010s, he observes that the economic downturn is forcing a reality-based approach where companies must finally 'show me the money' and build solid data foundations before pursuing AI.
key_points: AI investments often follow the 'underpants gnomes' pattern: AI → ? → profit, without clear paths to value
The core challenges in ML have always been engineering and data quality, not algorithms
Hype cycles led vendors to oversell AI capabilities and startups to add 'AI' to pitch decks purely for valuation bumps
Economic pressures are finally forcing organizations to approach data initiatives with realistic expectations
Success requires robust data architecture, mastering basics before AI, and building data-friendly culture
money_quotes: Five years ago, some companies made huge investments in AI without having enough high-quality foundational data to train and run the algorithms. That left executives underwhelmed by the results and disillusioned.
I still remember people claiming BI, SQL, and data warehousing were dead in the water because AI would make them obsolete in only a few years.

Vendors would oversell the AI capabilities of their products, often claiming to be doing AI when a quick look under the hood revealed a bunch of if-else statements, linear regression, or something similarly unsophisticated.
Even early on, I realized that ML algorithms were relatively easy compared to challenges such as scoping and framing the problem, getting a good dataset for training, hosting models, retraining, etc.

Succeeding with a data initiative is hard work. It requires building a solid foundation of robust data architecture and systems, getting good at the basics before you leap into AI, and fostering a data-friendly culture.
key_insight: AI success requires boring foundational work—quality data, solid engineering, and clear problem framing—not hype-driven investment in algorithms.
